Look, the aluminum frame of iPhones is anodized before being painted. Due to which some microscopic pores are formed on the surface of the frame and it is because of these pores that coloring of the frame becomes possible. title: ProRes ‘Almost’ RAW vs 12-Bit DCG RAW Video Shootout! (iPhone 17 Pro vs Pixel 10 Pro)
transcript: In this video, I'm going to share my findings  as to which is the best device for filmmakers,   the iPhone 17 in ProRes RAW or the Google  Pixel 10 Pro in 12-bit DCG RAW. The answer   is both more complicated and surprising than I'd  originally thought. From image quality, autofocus   and stabilization to ProRes RAW versus MotionCam  RAW workflows, file sizes, storage requirements,   accessories, durability, and more. This is the  ultimate deep dive you've been waiting for. First, let's start by addressing the  elephant in the room, ProRes RAW, which   is the industry-leading Apple developed video  codec that records all the information captured   by the camera sensor, provide unparalleled  flexibility in post-prouction. Like me,   you probably expected the iPhone 17 Pro to deliver  on the promise of raw video. And by raw video,   I mean unprocessed data capture directly from  the camera's sensor, devoid of any additional   image signal processing or ISP. And we didn't get  that. Apple still applies a ton of processing to   the data coming off the sensor before encoding it  to ProRes RAW. This means your noise reduction,   vignette correction, even enhanced dynamic range  via frame stacking is all still present. Is it   better than standard ProRes available on the 15  and 16 Pro? Yes, a bit. Does it deliver a great   image? Mostly, but is it raw video? No, it's not.  A better name is ProRes almost raw. Of course,   Apple baking in a ton of ISP has some  benefits like sparing you denoising in post,   but that defeats the whole purpose of raw video,  which should offer full control over your image in   post thanks to untouch raw sensor data, something  the Pixel 10 Pro delivers in spades using Motion   Cam Pro. So, how does ProRes RAW compare to true  12-bit raw video on the Pixel 10 Pro? Unlike the   iPhone motion cam pro captures the raw sensor  data and saves it in its proprietary MC RAW   video format, you can then convert this to DNG  raw for editing. This bypasses all the phone's   image signal processing and delivers a true raw  video format which is simply delightful. Whereas   ProRes RAW allows for ISO, exposure bias, detail,  noise, and temperature adjustments in Resolve,   RAW video from the Pixel 10 Pro offers a much more  comprehensive suite of camera raw adjustments like   highlight and shadow controls, saturation and  contrast, sharpness, and most impressively,   highlight recovery. This allows us to clip the  green channel at time of recording and restore   the image information from the unclipped red  and blue channels, which is incredibly useful   and delivers amazing dynamic range. On the iPhone  17 Pro, however, once your highlights are clipped,   they are gone forever. You can't retrieve that  information as ProRes RAW on the iPhone does   not support highlight recovery. And while the  iPhone certainly delivers reduced sharpening   in ProRes RAW versus non-Apple log formats,  it still appears to receive some additional   sharpening. Look how much more organic the  Pixel 10 looks with sharpness set to zero   versus the iPhone 17 Pro. And let's take a quick  look at EDR in play on the iPhone 17 Pro. If you   film in a high dynamic range scene with very  bright highlights with exposure set to auto,   we get dynamic range that is too good to be true.  That is until we lock exposure. You can see that   the frame stacking HDR ISP is suddenly disabled  and we instantly lose our highlight detail. Clear   proof that this is anything but raw video. On the  one hand, this is kind of a cool feature. Extra   dynamic range in auto mode, but it is bad for  consistency and should not be a feature of raw   video. And the other thing that struck me when  comparing the image out of both smartphones,   is how much richer the color information is on  the Pixel, especially in more subtle gradients   like the sky at dusk or in high contrast scenes  that feature a lot of highlight information. The   iPhone just doesn't capture everything that was  actually available to it. And it isn't just a   matter of increasing saturation either. The Pixel  consistently captures higher quality images that   are more representative of what I perceive with  my eye while filming. And lastly, let's compare   the file size of RAW in Motion Cam Pro verse  ProRes RAW and ProRes RAW HQ on the iPhone. A 1   minute recording on the Pixel 10 Pro comes in at  approximately 15 GB in MC RAW format. When this   is converted to the RAW DGs, the same clip is  19 GB when ready to edit. On the iPhone 17 Pro,   the same minute long recording clocks in at 7.88  GB in ProRes RAW and 12 GB in ProRes RAW HQ,   making the iPhone much more efficient for file  size. But the downside is there is less data to   work with, and it shows. So, when we compare the  raw video on the iPhone 17 Pro versus the Google   Pixel 10 Pro, the Pixel is the clear winner in  this round when it comes to pure image quality. Now, let's compare the quality of the glass on the  iPhone 17 Pro versus the Pixel 10 Pro. On paper,   the two devices stack up pretty closely with the  iPhone offering a 13 mm ultra wide lens, the 24 mm   main lens and a 100 mm telephoto lens, all using  48 megapixel sensors. The Pixel 10 Pro features a   48 MP 13 mm ultra-wide, a 50 megapixel 25mm main  lens, and a 48 megapixel 114 mm telephoto lens.   Both main lenses are excellent. The ultra-wide  on the Pixel is prone to heavier chromatic   aberration on the corners than the iPhone, and  the telly lenses are close, but I'd give the Pixel   the edge. It is 14 mm longer than the iPhone,  supports a much closer minimum focus distance,   and delivers gorgeous results. But the Pixel has  another trick up its sleeve thanks to MotionCam   Pro. Unlike on the iPhone 17 Pro, which does not  support in sensor zoom to provide additional focal   lengths in ProRes RAW, the Pixel 10 Pro delivers  a 51 mm and 228 mm lens, which is achieved by   cropping in on the sensor and has Apple improved  their signature lens flare, which has long   plagued the iPhone. Yes, the lens coating is much  improved on the 17 Pro. And what's more, Google   has achieved the impressive feat of beating Apple  when it comes to ugly lens flares, delivering a   pronounced green goblin on the main lens when  filming bright, undiffused light sources. Now, when it comes to image stabilization,  there is a clear winner here, the Pixel 10   Pro. It offers optical image stabilization on  all rear lenses when capturing in open gate raw,   whereas the iPhone 17 Pro disables  stabilization on all lenses in ProRes   RAW. Not even sensor shift is available on  the iPhone. Handheld, this is literally the   difference between getting usable footage and  shots only your trash can will get to enjoy,   especially on the telly lens, which  is simply unusable on the iPhone. When it comes to low light performance, you  could say this is where the iPhone's almost RAW   with heavy noise reduction performed in camera  really shines. You can get an incredibly clean   image straight out of camera, but of course,  you're deprived of the ability to denoise in   post using something like Resolve or Neat Video.  The Pixel 10 Pro is no slouch either here, thanks   to DCG being enabled on the sensor when using the  main wide lens, and you get very good low light   performance and a lot more color information  in the shadows when compared to the iPhone.   But as you would expect, there is a lot more noise  present as this is free from denoising. It's hard   to pick a winner here because you're comparing  apples to oranges. It comes down to whether you   want full control over denoising in post or a  super clean image out of camera. Both are strong   performers here with the Pixel giving the most  pure image with more chroma information intact. Next, let's talk about some key specs as  they pertain to video such as frame rates,   sustain performance, and thermals. And let's start  with resolution. On the iPhone 17 Pro, we get a   slightly higher max resolution of 4224x3024. And  on the Pixel 10 Pro, we get 4080x3072. ProRes RAW   is only supported when capturing to an external  SSD on the iPhone, which is a key limitation,   whereas RAW capture on the Pixel is supported  both internally and externally to an SSD, which   is great. When it comes to record times, I've  successfully recorded over 1 hour of 4K ProRes   RAW HQ at 120 fps to my external SSD, and no doubt  it would go for much longer if I had the patience.   On the iPhone, you're simply limited by available  storage on your SSD. On the Pixel 10 Pro, when   recording externally to the same SSD, I can record  continuously for a max of about 20 minutes in RAW   before I get dropped frames and IO bottlenecks in  cool conditions. While filming this video, I've   had dropped frames when recording internally after  just 5 minutes. So, ambient temperatures play a   big role in the sort of performance you can expect  from the Pixel. For extended record times, the   iPhone 17 Pro is the clear winner. When it comes  to max supported frame rates, again, the iPhone 17   Pro wins here with support for 60 fps in open gate  and 120 fps in 4K. The Pixel 10 Pro is limited to   a max of 30 fps in OpenGate at 4K and 120 fps  at 1080p in 10- bit RAW without DCG. However,   it will drop frames after 5 to 10 seconds, so it  isn't a reliable high-speed filmmaking option.   Something else to be aware of on the Pixel 10 Pro  is the laser detect autofocus module positioned   directly next to the wide lens. This is constantly  firing and will be reflected on close objects and   more problematically on your ND filter or lens.  This means you'll need to cover it with Blu-Tack   or electrical tape if you intend to use an ND  filter. The iPhone 17 doesn't have this issue,   nor does any other Android device I've tested.  And speaking of autofocus, even when the LDAF is   uncovered on the Pixel 10 Pro, it still performs  significantly worse than the iPhone, which does a   much better job. Unless you are dead center in the  frame, the Pixel 10 just won't focus on you. So,   where possible on the Pixel, make sure you use  manual focus, and of course, Motion Cam Pro   supports focus peaking to nail this. But if you're  a one-man band like yours truly that relies on   autofocus, the iPhone is the winner here, too. And  when it comes to general performance, the Pixel is   a serious underachiever. Even in just mildly warm  conditions, the CPU will throttle after just 5 to   10 minutes of use when filming in raw, which leads  to instabilities in capture. The iPhone 17 Pro,   even the 15 Pro actually, offers flawless sustain  performance even in really warm environments. That   puts the Pixel to shame. And lastly, what makes  the Pixel 10 Pro so special is it's the first   phone to unlock dual conversion gain or DCG on the  sensor out of the box. I've made a whole video on   why this is amazing that you can watch, but just  think of this as dual output gain that you'll find   on high-end cinema cameras where it can capture  two analog exposures simultaneously and merge them   for improved noise and expanded dynamic range.  But not to be confused with HDR frame stacking,   there is no ghosting present with DCG. So in this  regard, the Pixel 10 Pro is in a league of its   own. But due to all the other issues I've just  outlined, imagine what would happen if Xiaomi,   Samsung, or Vivo unlock DCG on their sensors  without the performance issues that plague   the Pixel. So, when it comes to supportive frame  rates, sustained record times, and performance in   general, the iPhone 17 Pro absolutely destroys the  Pixel in this round when it comes to key specs. When it comes to film making accessories like  cases, tripod mounts, ND filters, and lenses,   the iPhone 17 Pro is the clear winner by a country  mile. In fact, no manufacturer currently makes a   dedicated solution for the Pixel 10 Pro, although  I hope that will change in the future. On the   iPhone, we get dozens of options from companies  like Mman, Small Rig, and my personal favorite,   Freewell. This makes rigging out your phone easy  with professional gear like their hybrid V&D   filter solution that even supports drop-in filters  with the Pro Filmmaker case.
